feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis

paragraphs.text: Directorate-General for Justice and Consumers European Commission 1049 Bruxelles/Brussel Belgium
24 October 2025
Re: Digital Fairness Act public consultation
Contributors:
Prof. Dr. Maximilian Becker (professor, Universität Siegen) Dr. Catalina Goanta (associate professor, Utrecht University; Mercator fellow, Universität Siegen)
To whom it may concern,

This is a response to the solicitation for public comments of the European Commission, relating to the Digital Fairness Act (DFA). Our feedback reflects insights from two researchers pertaining to the Special Collaborative Research Centre (CRC) 1187 "Media of Cooperation" at the University of Siegen.
This submission aims to provide academic guidance for the development of the Digital Fairness Act on two specific topics:
The simplification of business models on digital markets.
Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis: enforcement-facing information duties.
The simplification of business models on digital markets

There is a lot of ongoing debate around the simplification of regulatory regimes in the European Union, such as the GDPR (EDPB 2025, ELI 2025). While it is necessary to understand how to make the complex web of laws applicable to digital markets simpler, this public debate should also include another aspect of digital markets that can benefit from simplification: business models. The rise of opaque business models in the datafied web (Helmond 2025) has led to an equally opaque ecosystem of tracking and profiling. To eventually benefit consumers – assuming that ideally this would not entail data protection issues – such an ecosystem would have to deliver better business solutions for consumers: better products and better services. However, technology experts such as Cory Doctorow have highlighted that a lot of Internet services are suffering from what he calls 'enshittification' (Doctorow 2023), namely the worsening of service quality not only for consumer but also for their
U
Universität Siegen
Media
of
Cooperation

business users. Empirical evidence of this can be found even in this call for feedback itself, where thousands of consumers have joined forces to popularize the 'Stop Killing Games' campaign showing the worsening of gaming services. This problem is so wide spread that a total of 1,448,270 EU consumers signed the equivalent 'Stop Destroying Videogames' citizens' initiative. Another example is the integration of social commerce and streaming monetization models on social media platforms, who become, as Aade puts it, 'social marketplaces' (Aade 2025). If a platform like TikTok combines video ads with shopping, consumers might find advertising and e-commerce quite straightforward by themselves; however, not the same can be said about the convoluted additional products, such as the search recommendations which are turning TikTok also into a search engine (Annabell et al. 2025a). Currently, most of the commercial features arising out of the servitization of additional digital content lead to associated network of contracts that are so complex that they constitute a scientific research topic in their own right. TikTok alone has a whopping 85 consumer-facing documents such as community guidelines, terms of service for various services, and additional policies (Annabell et al. 2025b). In short, simplification here means 'don't hide dishonesty in complexity'.
The simplification of business models can be achieved in at least two ways:

a. By specifically prohibiting certain practices (e.g. targeted advertising on children, dark patterns such as subscription traps); and b. By increasing the legal risks associated with some business models (e.g. the use of in-app currencies and virtual items). Transparency obligations should also include the inherent transparency of the business model itself, not just its explanation.

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: The consumer acquis, just as other EU legal acts in the digital acquis, such as the GDPR, are primarily based on preserving consumer autonomy. A traditional approach for this policy goal has been the focus on consent, which is however deeply challenged in a landscape of increasing complexity and opacity in business practices (Lemley 2025). Since an overhaul of the consent model is currently practically impossible (and most likely doctrinally undesirable) at EU policy level, we suggest that the EU consumer acquis complement its established information duties with enforcement-facing information duties. Instead of focusing on the information of consumers, which is arguably difficult to achieve since consumers do not read terms of service and generally do not understand the complex infrastructures of complex online platforms, information duties should become geared towards enforcement agencies.

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: Media of Cooperation

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: This point is very much linked to the complexity of business models identified in our first point. If business models are inherently incomprehensible or non-transparent, this should be a disadvantage for the providers, not the users. This would mean a shift from formal and informational requirements (see Art. 5 et seq. CRD) to material, business-model-related requirements.

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: With computational assistance, information duties can be examined for compliance purposes by any relevant stakeholders, including public authorities, civil society and competitors. Pałka et al. have already made this argument in the context of data protection and privacy policies, arguing that LLMs can be used by data subjects and other actors to extract needed information (Pałka, Lagioia, Liepina, Lippi and Sartor 2025). The expected outcome of such a shift in the goal of information duties can be achieving fairer defaults for consumers.

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: This can entail two concrete objectives:

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: a. Mandating online platforms to systematize their existing information duties and making them machine-readable, such as through specific data formats. Systematization should take into account whether information is relevant during a pre-contractual phase, a contractual phase or a post- contractual phase. Currently, information duties arise from various fields of law and deal with different phases, but platform practices make this even more complex due to the nature of additional/subsequent services.

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: b. Adopting new information duties for online platforms, inspired by the DSA. For instance, Art. 24 of the DSA asks online platforms to share different categories of information that were previously unknown. While transparency may not be an effective replacement for direct accountability, it can lead to accountability by allowing for the triangulation of evidence around non-compliance. Here, the main regulatory question should be: what are the relevant categories of consumer information that platforms should publish?

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: The transparency thus created, could form the basis for new regulatory approaches or a political debate – the aim is not to inform users indirectly via experts, but to enable new regulation. Making contracts/contractual networks and data processing transparent to professionals could, on the one hand, help uncover problematic practices. This is meant as a complementary, not an alternative form of transparency. Experts, for instance, should be able to download all relevant information on contracts, FAQs and personal data processing by websites/platforms, in addition to existing information obligations. This would facilitate not only enforcement by authorities but also enforcement by associations and competitors (Becker 2025).

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: Media of Cooperation

section_path: Transparency and accountability obligations for intermediaries in the consumer acquis
paragraphs.text: This enforcement transparency would support the first proposal - which calls for business models and their associated contracts to be inherently understandable and transparent for users - both in its implementation and in its future development.
